diagnoses.diagnosis: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) — Hyperandrogenic, Insulin-Resistant Phenotype
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Classic hyperandrogenic PCOS: total testosterone 82 ng/dL (ref 15-70) elevated, SHBG suppressed at 16 nmol/L (ref 18-144) driven by hyperinsulinemia, DHEA-S 385 µg/dL (ref 45-320) elevated (adrenal androgen contribution), and AMH 4.8 ng/mL (ref 1.0-3.5) consistent with polycystic ovarian morphology. LH 14.8 mIU/mL with FSH 4.2 gives an LH:FSH ratio ~3.5:1. Insulin resistance is the primary driver — fasting insulin 32.1 µIU/mL with fasting glucose 110 mg/dL yields an estimated HOMA-IR of 8.7, and TG/HDL ratio 4.6 corroborates. Estradiol 22 pg/mL is low for follicular phase; prolactin 26.4 ng/mL is high-normal. Irregular periods since age 22, family history of PCOS (mother and sister) and T2D (mother), South Asian lineage (highest PCOS prevalence), and stated fertility goals reinforce the diagnosis.

diagnoses.diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Fasting insulin 32.1 µIU/mL (ref 2.6-24.9) with glucose 110 mg/dL gives an estimated HOMA-IR of 8.7 (optimal <1.5), confirming severe insulin resistance; HbA1c 6.1% and glucose 110 mg/dL indicate pre-diabetes. The lipid pattern is the insulin-resistance atherogenic phenotype: triglycerides 192 mg/dL, HDL 42 mg/dL (low for females), TG/HDL ratio 4.6, ApoB 118 mg/dL, LDL particle number 1280 nmol/L, and small dense LDL 48 mg/dL (ref <35). Uric acid 5.8 mg/dL sits at the upper limit and morning cortisol 21.5 µg/dL adds HPA-axis-driven insulin resistance. Family history of T2D and hyperlipidemia plus South Asian lineage indicate high progression risk without intervention.

diagnoses.diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis (NAFLD) and Hypomagnesemia
diagnoses.clinical_rationale: Vitamin D 19 ng/mL is insufficient (ref 30-100), with intact PTH 58 pg/mL at the upper end (compensatory). ALT 64 U/L (ref 7-56) exceeds AST 46 U/L (ref 10-40) — an ALT-dominant pattern typical of early insulin-resistance-driven hepatic fat deposition (NAFLD). hs-CRP 3.9 mg/L indicates active low-grade systemic inflammation. Serum magnesium 1.5 mg/dL is below range; magnesium is a required cofactor for insulin receptor signaling, so its depletion compounds the insulin resistance. Elevated cortisol with 6-7 hours of sleep and a desk job further sustains the inflammatory, insulin-resistant state.

foods_to_eat.food: Chia and ground flaxseed (chía, linaza molida)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Flaxseed lignans support healthy hormone metabolism and can lower circulating male-pattern hormones, while the soluble fiber and omega-3 ALA in both slow sugar absorption and reduce the insulin your body needs after meals.
Two cups of spearmint tea daily has been shown to gently lower male-pattern hormones and reduce related skin and hair symptoms. It is simple, accessible, and caffeine-free.

foods_to_eat.food: Lentils, chickpeas and black beans (lentejas, garbanzos, frijoles negros)
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Low-glycemic legumes deliver fiber and plant protein that keep blood sugar steady between meals, and they supply magnesium, which your level shows is low (1.5 mg/dL).

foods_to_eat.food: Fatty fish — salmon and sardines (salmón, sardinas)
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: The EPA and DHA in oily fish lower triglycerides (yours are 192 mg/dL), calm inflammation (hs-CRP 3.9 mg/L), and improve how your cells respond to insulin.
Walnuts have been shown to raise SHBG — the protein that binds excess hormones, and which is low in your results (16 nmol/L) — while improving cholesterol balance.

foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Its monounsaturated fats help raise your protective HDL cholesterol (currently low at 42 mg/dL) and improve insulin sensitivity, and it adds potassium and B vitamins.

foods_to_eat.food: Leafy greens — spinach, chard, kale (espinaca, acelga, col rizada)
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: These are rich in magnesium, which supports insulin signaling and is low in your labs, and in folate, which matters for fertility. Their glycemic load is very low.

foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Ceylon cinnamon can improve insulin sensitivity and blunt the blood-sugar rise after meals, which helps with your fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL.
They provide zinc and magnesium that support healthy hormone production and insulin function, plus plant protein and omega-3 ALA.

foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: These complete plant proteins have a moderate glycemic impact and are gluten-free, providing B vitamins and minerals needed for hormone synthesis without spiking blood sugar.

foods_to_eat.quantity_frequency: 1/2 cup cooked 3-4x per week or as tea
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: It contains compounds that mimic insulin's action and improve how your cells take up glucose — a traditional South Asian and Latin American remedy for blood sugar.

foods_to_eat.food: Turmeric with black pepper (cúrcuma con pimienta negra)
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Curcumin lowers inflammation (hs-CRP 3.9 mg/L), supports the liver, and improves insulin sensitivity; black pepper makes it far easier to absorb.

foods_to_eat.food: Berries — blueberries, strawberries, blackberries (arándanos, fresas, moras)
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: These low-sugar fruits are rich in polyphenols that improve insulin response and reduce inflammation, and their vitamin C supports stress-hormone balance.

foods_to_eat.food: Coconut yogurt with live cultures (yogur de coco con probióticos)
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: A dairy-free probiotic that respects your dairy intolerance; a healthier gut microbiome supports hormone balance and improves insulin sensitivity.

foods_to_eat.food: Extra virgin olive oil (aceite de oliva extra virgen)
foods_to_eat.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
foods_to_eat.why_it_helps: Its anti-inflammatory polyphenols lower hs-CRP, improve cholesterol balance, and ease the strain reflected in your liver enzymes (ALT 64, AST 46).

foods_to_avoid.food: Refined carbohydrates — white rice, white bread, refined-flour snacks (arroz blanco, pan blanco, botanas)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Maximum 1 small serving daily; replace with legumes and whole grains
foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Your fasting insulin (32.1) shows your cells are already working hard to manage sugar. Each refined-carb spike adds to that excess insulin, which in turn drives the hormone imbalance behind your other results.
You have documented dairy intolerance, and dairy also raises insulin and growth-signaling hormones that can worsen your hormone balance. Use unsweetened coconut, oat, or almond alternatives.

foods_to_avoid.food: Sweetened beverages — sodas, packaged juices, sweetened teas (refrescos, jugos envasados, aguas frescas con azúcar)
foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Liquid sugar causes the fastest blood-sugar spikes and raises triglycerides (yours are 192 mg/dL). These drinks give the least fullness for the most sugar.

foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Your habit of frequent snacking keeps insulin elevated all day. Shifting to three structured meals lets insulin fall between meals, which is when your hormones can rebalance.

foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Fried foods create inflammatory compounds that raise hs-CRP (3.9 mg/L) and add to the strain shown in your liver enzymes.

foods_to_avoid.food: High-glycemic fruits in large portions — mango, pineapple, ripe banana (mango, piña, plátano maduro)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: 1/2 cup maximum, always paired with protein or fat
foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Eaten alone or in large amounts they spike blood sugar. Pairing a small portion with nuts or seeds blunts that rise.

foods_to_avoid.food: Commercial baked goods and sweets (galletas, pasteles, pan dulce)
foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: These combine refined flour, sugar, and low-quality fats — the strongest trigger for the insulin and triglyceride elevations in your labs.

foods_to_avoid.food: Seed oils high in omega-6 — corn, sunflower, soybean (aceites de maíz, girasol, soya)
foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: Replace with olive oil or avocado oil
foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Too much omega-6 fat tips the balance toward inflammation, working against the improvement you want in your hs-CRP and liver markers.

foods_to_avoid.food: Excess caffeine, especially afternoon and evening
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: Your morning cortisol is already elevated (21.5 µg/dL). Extra caffeine raises it further, which makes it harder for your body to manage blood sugar and disturbs the 6-7 hours of sleep you currently get.

foods_to_avoid.reduction_target: No more than 1-2 drinks per week
foods_to_avoid.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
foods_to_avoid.why_to_avoid: You currently drink 2-3 times per week. Alcohol adds direct strain to the liver (your ALT and AST are elevated) and disrupts hormone processing.

exercise_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Building muscle is the single most powerful way to improve how your body handles blood sugar — more muscle means more places to store glucose without extra insulin. It also helps balance your hormones. Focus on compound moves like squats, lunges, and rows.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: No medical contraindications. Begin with bodyweight and light weights; your existing yoga practice gives you a good movement foundation.

exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: Short high-intensity intervals have been shown to improve insulin sensitivity and help regulate cycles more than steady cardio alone — and they fit a busy desk-job schedule.
exercise_recommendations.safety_notes: Start with longer recovery intervals. Stop if you feel lightheaded or have chest discomfort.

exercise_recommendations.exercise_type: Post-meal walking and yoga (build on your current practice)
exercise_recommendations.duration: 20-30 min walking after meals; 30-45 min yoga
exercise_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
exercise_recommendations.why_it_helps: A short walk after meals lowers the blood-sugar spike by 20-30%. Yoga lowers cortisol (currently elevated at 21.5 µg/dL), which directly improves insulin sensitivity — your existing yoga habit is genuinely valuable, so keep it.

supplement_recommendations.dosage_range: 4,000 mg myo-inositol + 100 mg D-chiro-inositol daily, split into 2 doses
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: The most evidence-based supplement for your pattern. It improves insulin sensitivity, helps lower excess male-pattern hormones, raises SHBG (yours is low at 16 nmol/L), and supports more regular cycles and fertility.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: Very well tolerated, and you take no medications it interacts with. Start at 2 g and titrate up.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Inositol is available in pharmacies and health food stores across Latin America.

supplement_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Your vitamin D is 19 ng/mL (insufficient). Bringing it to 50-60 improves insulin sensitivity, supports hormone balance, and lowers inflammation.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interactions with your current routine. Retest vitamin D in 10-12 weeks.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: D3 is widely available in LATAM pharmacies; K2 (MK-7) is found in health food stores — D3 alone is acceptable if K2 is hard to source.

supplement_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Vitamin D Insufficiency with Early Hepatic Steatosis and Hypomagnesemia
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Your serum magnesium is low (1.5 mg/dL), and magnesium is required for insulin to work properly. It also lowers cortisol and improves the quality of your sleep.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interactions; compatible with your Biotin supplement.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Magnesium Glycinate is in health food stores; Magnesium Citrate is a widely available pharmacy alternative.

supplement_recommendations.supplement_name: Omega-3 Fish Oil or Algae Omega-3 (EPA + DHA)
supplement_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Lowers your triglycerides (192 mg/dL) and hs-CRP (3.9 mg/L), improves insulin sensitivity, and supports hormone and cycle health.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: No interactions with your current routine. Choose an algae-based version if you prefer to avoid fish.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Fish oil is widely available; algae-based omega-3 is in health food stores.

supplement_recommendations.target_diagnosis: Severe Insulin Resistance with Pre-Diabetic Atherogenic Dyslipidemia
supplement_recommendations.why_it_helps: Activates an energy-sensing pathway (AMPK) that improves insulin sensitivity, with studies showing benefit for blood sugar and this specific hormone pattern. Your fasting glucose, HbA1c, and cholesterol should all improve.
supplement_recommendations.safety_note: You take no medications it interacts with, but start at 500 mg once daily and titrate up over 2 weeks; watch for signs of low blood sugar.
supplement_recommendations.local_availability_note: Available in health food stores (tiendas naturistas) throughout Latin America.

patient_summary: Your panel reviewed 69 markers across four panels, with 22 values outside the normal range clustered in your reproductive hormones, blood-sugar handling, and cholesterol balance. The results show your body is producing more insulin than it should to keep blood sugar steady (fasting insulin 32.1, glucose 110, HbA1c 6.1%), and this is closely linked to a hormone imbalance — elevated male-pattern hormones with a low binding protein (SHBG) — as well as a cholesterol pattern with high triglycerides and low protective HDL. Your vitamin D is low (19 ng/mL), your magnesium is below range, and your liver and inflammation markers are mildly elevated, all of which tie back to the same root of insulin overwork. The most impactful first steps are starting myo-inositol with D-chiro-inositol, vitamin D3, and magnesium, cutting out frequent snacking, refined carbohydrates, and dairy, and adding resistance training 3-4 times per week. With consistent changes over about three months these markers typically improve substantially; repeat hormone and metabolic testing at 3 months is recommended to track progress.

system_summaries.summary: Your complete blood count is entirely within normal range, with no signs of anemia or infection. Iron stores are healthy, with ferritin at 45 ng/mL. No action is needed here.

Your blood-sugar markers show your body is working hard to manage glucose: fasting insulin is high at 32.1 µIU/mL, fasting glucose is 110 mg/dL, and HbA1c is 6.1%, which together show your body is in the early stages of struggling to keep blood sugar in the ideal range. Uric acid is at the top of the normal range. This is the central finding driving several of your other results, and it responds well to the diet, exercise, and supplement steps in your plan.

Your cholesterol profile shows a pattern linked to blood-sugar handling: triglycerides 192 mg/dL, HDL low at 42 mg/dL, TG/HDL ratio 4.6, ApoB 118 mg/dL, LDL particle number 1280, and small dense LDL 48 mg/dL. Inflammation, measured by hs-CRP, is elevated at 3.9 mg/L. Lp(a), homocysteine, and NT-proBNP are all normal. These markers typically improve substantially as insulin sensitivity improves through the recommended changes.

Your liver enzymes are mildly elevated — ALT 64 U/L and AST 46 U/L — which suggests the liver is under some extra strain, commonly from early fat build-up linked to how your body is handling blood sugar. Bilirubin, albumin, and total protein are all normal. Reducing refined carbohydrates, fried foods, and alcohol, plus improving insulin sensitivity, will help bring these enzymes down.
Your kidney markers are all normal — creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL, and a normal urinalysis — indicating healthy kidney function at this time.

Most electrolytes are normal, but magnesium is below range at 1.5 mg/dL. This matters because magnesium is needed for insulin to work well, so correcting it through leafy greens, nuts, seeds, and a magnesium supplement directly supports your blood-sugar goals.
Your TSH is 2.8 mIU/mL, well within the normal range, indicating your thyroid is functioning normally based on this screening marker.

Your vitamin D is low at 19 ng/mL, which can worsen insulin sensitivity, hormone balance, and inflammation. Your parathyroid hormone (PTH) is at the upper end of normal at 58 pg/mL, a common response to low vitamin D. Restoring vitamin D is a priority and is addressed by the supplement plan.

Your oxidative-stress marker (8-OHdG) is at the upper edge of normal at 0.48 ng/mL, consistent with the mild inflammation seen elsewhere in your results. The anti-inflammatory foods and improved insulin sensitivity in your plan should help lower this over time.

Your hormone panel shows a clear imbalance: total testosterone is elevated at 82 ng/dL, DHEA-S is high at 385 µg/dL, and SHBG — the protein that binds excess hormones — is low at 16 nmol/L, so more active hormone is circulating. LH is elevated relative to FSH, AMH is high at 4.8 ng/mL, and morning cortisol is mildly elevated at 21.5 µg/dL. The low SHBG is directly driven by high insulin, so improving insulin sensitivity will raise SHBG and reduce the excess hormone effect; prolactin is high-normal and worth rechecking.
